rulings.Case Title: SCOTT J. MANDELL ET AL VS. GUERNEVILLE BUSINESS CORPORATION, INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Reopen Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, January 23, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF ARTHUR FONDEN's Motion To Reopen Discovery Pro Tem Judge Jason Yurasek, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Stipulation and Order entered 01.18.19. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jyjudgeprotem@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KEVIN NEALAN VS. LEDCOR CONSTRUCTION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion By Defendant Ledcor Construction, Inc.S To Compel Plaintiff To Provide Further Responses To (1) Supplemental Interrogatories And (2) Supplemental Request For Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, January 23, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT LEDCOR CONSTRUCTION, INC's Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Provide Further Responses To (1) Supplemental Interrogatories And (2) Supplemental Request For Production Of Documents Pro Tem Judge Jason Yurasek, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Discovery Act is clear: "The party propounding the interrogatory may move for an order compelling response to the interrogatory." See CCP 2030.290(b). "The party making the demand may move for an order compelling response to the demand." See CCP 2031.300(b). The supplemental discovery at issue in this motion was served by another party, and therefore Ledcor is not the "propounding party." The motion is therefore denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jyjudgeprotem@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SAN SAN AUNG VS. REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT SAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants' Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of Issues (Added For Purposes Of Entering The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Defendants' Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of Issues *** TENTATIVE RULING - PART 2 OF 2 *** Although she admitted that no sexual behavior occurred ("did he do anything sexual to you?"), she also testified that Mr. Hermogeno continued to ask her inappropriate personal questions, refused to allow Ms. Aung to visit the restroom as needed, and followed her to the restroom. Similarly, Ms. Aung testified that after a 2005 incident in which Kevin Tucker hugged her tightly and was suspended as a result, he, too, followed her to the restroom. Contrary to defendants' characterization of the conduct following the 2004 and 2005 incidents as non-actionable personnel management decisions, the allegedly harassing or retaliatory conduct need not be overtly sexual. (See Dominguez, 168 Cal.App.4th at 723-24 [reasonable inference that supervisor's campaign to make employee's work life miserable was "just another way for [supervisor] to harass [plaintiff] about her sexual orientation without expressly saying so"]; Birschstein, 92 Cal.App.4th at 1001-02 [rejecting employer's argument that repeated acts of staring at a fellow worker could not qualify as actionable sexual harassment as a matter of law, even though they were not sexually suggestive].) Further, the same evidence cited above gives rise to triable issues of fact as to whether the regular harassment to which Ms. Aung claims she has been subjected since the 2004 and 2005 incidents constitutes a concerted pattern of harassment of a repeated, routine, or generalized nature, rather than merely isolated, sporadic, or trivial incidents. (Fuentes v. AutoZone, Inc. (2011) 200 Cal.App.4th 1221, 1226-27.) Ms. Aung has not failed to exhaust her administrative remedies. In her DFEH charge she alleged she had been subject to discrimination and harassment on the basis of sex, among other grounds. While that charge did not specifically refer to the 2004 and 2005 incidents, it did refer to the conduct summarized above, and specifically alleged that she had been asked impoermissible non-job-related questions and denied a work environment free of discrimination and/or retaliation. Thus, her claims in the instant lawsuit are like and reasonably related to those in her DFEH charge, and the 2004 and 2005 incidents were likely to be uncovered in the course of a DFEH investigation. (Okoli v. Lockheed Technical Operations Co. (1995) 36 Cal.App.4th 1607, 1614-17.) Finally, contrary to defendants' argument that Ms. Aung did not suffer an adverse employment action, a trier of fact could find that after Ms. Aung complained about the 2004 and 2005 incidents, she was subjected to conduct by her supervisors that had "a detrimental and substantial effect" on her employment, precluding summary adjudication of her cause of action for retaliation. (Aung Depo. pp. 95, 107, 124; Aung Decl. 8, 9, 10, 12). (See Akers v. County of San Diego (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 1441, 1455.) Because there are triable issues of fact as to the underlying claims of sexual harassment and retaliation, summary adjudication of the second cause of action for failure to prevent harassment and retaliation is also denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Aung is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** END OF TENTATIVE RULING *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SAN SAN AUNG VS. REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT SAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of Issues; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, January 23, 2019, line 6. *** TENTATIVE RULING - PART 1 OF 2 *** DEFENDANTS JESSEE HERMOGENO, THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of Issues. Judge Schulman discloses that he previously represented The Regents of the University of California. This will not affect Judge Schulman's ability to be fair and impartial in deciding this case. Defendants The Regents of the University of California and Jessee Hermogeno's motions for summary judgment and alternative motion for summary adjudication are denied. Plaintiff's counsel is admonished that the separate statement submitted with the opposition papers does not comply with the requirements for a separate statement set forth in Code Civ. Proc., § 437c(b)(3) and California Rules of Court, Rule 3.1350(f)(3), in that the material facts that plaintiff contends are disputed are not followed by a reference to the supporting evidence. In addition, there is no proof of service of the opposition papers on defendants. However, because the evidence submitted with the opposition is a single five-page declaration and defendants have not been unduly prejudiced, the court exercises its discretion to decide the motion on the merits. Defendants have failed to meet their burden to show they are entitled to summary adjudication of the first cause of action for sexual harassment on the ground that it is barred by the one-year statute of limitations contained in Government Code section 12960 because there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether a continuing violation exists. (See Dominguez v. Washington Mutual Bank (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 714, 720-24 [fact question regarding continuing violation precluded summary judgment in favor of employer on limitations grounds]; Birschtein v. New United Motor Mfg., Inc. (2001) 92 Cal.App.4th 994, 1005-06 [same].) Under the continuing violation doctrine, a FEHA complaint is timely if discriminatory practices occurring outside the limitations period continued into that period. A continuing violation exists if: (1) the conduct occurring within the limitations period is similar in kind to the conduct that falls outside the period; (2) the conduct was reasonably frequent; and (3) it had not yet acquired a degree of permanence. (Dominguez, 168 Cal.App.4th at 721.) Here, there are triable issues of fact as to whether Mr. Hermogeno engaged in continued sexual harassment or retaliation against Ms. Aung following the 2004 incident in which, after asking Ms. Aung inappropriate personal questions, he attempted to kiss her. In particular, in her declaration, Ms. Aung states that "for most of the past 14 years" after that incident she has been treated "extremely poorly" by her supervisors, including that "for years" Mr. Hermogeno has refused to give her permission to take a bathroom break, or has followed her to the bathroom; and that since 2014, Mr. Hermogeno has "harassed [her] regularly" by often making lewd and suggestive comments to her and sometimes taking photographs of her without her permission. (Aung Decl. 7, 8, 12.) Likewise, Ms. Aung testified at deposition that Mr. Hermogeno has refused to allow her to visit the restroom as needed, resulting on one occasion in her urinating in her clothes, and followed her to the restroom. (Aung Depo. 95:2-21, 96:1-14, 16-23; 97:11.) Defendant's reliance on the D'Amico rule (D'Amico v. Board of Medical Examiners (1974) 11 Cal.3d 1, 21) is misplaced. Ms. Aung's deposition answers, read in the context of her entire testimony, do not establish that she admitted no actionable harassment occurred after 2005. *** TENTATIVE RULING CONTINUES IN PART 2 OF 2 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL BIELOUS VS. ANTHONY K. NGAI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Default And Default Judgment, If Entered: Ccp Sec. 473(B); Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Anthony K. Ngai, Exhibts A-K, Attached Thereto; Declaration Of Jacqueline M. Phillips, Exhibits 1-4, Attached Thereto
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, January 23, 2019, line 7. DEFENDANT ANTHONY NGAI'S Motion To Set Aside Default And Default Judgment, If Entered: Ccp Sec. 473(B). Defendant Anthony K. Ngai's motion to set aside the default entered on June 21, 2018 is denied. Mr. Ngai has not made a showing that the default was taken against him through his mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect. There is undisputed evidence that after he was personally served with the summons and complaint, Mr. Ngai retained counsel, who specifically informed plaintiff's counsel that "Mr. Ngai does not intend to respond to your client's complaint." Further, Mr. Ngai does not deny that in June 2018, he and his counsel requested and received a copy of defendant's request for entry of default. He has not shown that he exercised diligence in seeking relief from default within a reasonable period of time after being served with the request for entry of default. (See Stafford v. Allstate Ins. Co. (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 1174, 1183-84 [trial court abused its discretion by granting relief where defendant waited to file its motion until the last day of the six-month limitation period provided by section 473 without an adequate explanation to justify the prolonged delay].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SULAIMAN AKRAMI VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, January 23, 2019, line 10. DEFENDANT C. MONDAVI & FAMILY'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant C. Mondavi & Family dba Charles Krug Winery's demurrer to the first cause of action for general negligence in plaintiff's first amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff has failed to allege facts showing a special relationship that would support a duty on the part of defendant C. Mondavi & Family to protect plaintiff from harmful conduct of third parties or that any breach of such duty was a substantial cause of plaintiff's injuries. (See Wise v. Superior Court (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 1008, 1013 ["A complaint which lacks facts to show that a duty of care was owed is fatally defective . . . . In general, one owes no duty to control the conduct of a third person to prevent him from causing physical harm to another, absent a special relationship between the defendant and either the person whose conduct needs to be controlled or the foreseeable victim of that conduct."].) Further, plaintiff has not alleged any basis to overcome the statutory immunity set forth in Business & Professions Code section 25602 ["No person who sells, furnishes, gives, or causes to be sold, furnished, or given away, any alcoholic beverage pursuant to subdivision (a) of this section shall be civilly liable to any injured person or the estate of such person for injuries inflicted on that person as a result of intoxication by the consumer of such alcoholic beverage"]; see Ennabe v. Manosa (2014) 58 Cal.4th 697, 722 ("Where injuries are proximately caused by excess alcohol consumption, our Legislature has carefully balanced the interests involved and settled on a rule generally precluding liability for those who provide alcoholic beverages, on the ground that 'the consumption of alcoholic beverages rather than the serving of alcoholic beverages [is] the proximate cause of injuries inflicted upon another by an intoxicated person.'"]; Leong v. San Francisco Parking, Inc. (1991) 235 Cal.App.3d 827, 831-34. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SULAIMAN AKRAMI VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, January 23, 2019, line 10. DEFENDANT C. MONDAVI & FAMILY'S MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant C. Mondavi & Family dba Charles Krug Winer's motion to strike the prayers for exemplary damages and attorney's fees in the first amended complaint is granted. In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud or malice. Civ. Code § 3294(a). "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1). The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Further, plaintiff has failed to allege any statutory or contractual basis for an award of attorney's fees. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
